content.problemStatement: Given N separate integer points on the Cartesian plane satisfying: there is no any three of them sharing a same X-coordinate. Your task is to count the number of rectangles (whose edges parrallel to the axes) created from any four of given points.
constraints: No three points have the same X‑coordinate
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of points. The next N lines each contain two integers x and y (|x|,|y| ≤ 10^9) describing a point. No three points share the same X‑coordinate.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of axis‑parallel rectangles that can be formed using the given points.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The only rectangle is formed by the points (0,0), (0,2), (2,0), (2,2).
No rectangles can be formed with the given points.

solution.approach:
For a rectangle we need two distinct X‑coordinates and two distinct Y‑coordinates such that all four corner points exist. Fix a Y‑coordinate (a horizontal line). All points that lie on this line give a set of X‑coordinates. Every unordered pair of X‑coordinates on the same line can serve as the top (or bottom) side of a rectangle. If the same unordered pair of X‑coordinates appears on k different Y‑lines, we can choose any two of those lines to form a rectangle, contributing C(k,2) = k·(k‑1)/2 rectangles. Therefore we:
1. Iterate over all points and group them by Y (using a map from Y to a vector of X).
2. For each Y‑group, for every unordered pair (x1,x2) (x1 < x2) increase a global counter for that pair.
3. After processing all Y‑groups, for each pair count c, add c·(c‑1)/2 to the answer.
The map key can be a 64‑bit integer built from the two X values (e.g., (int64)x1<<32 | (uint32)x2). The algorithm runs in O(N^2) worst‑case (N ≤ 1000) which is easily fast enough, and uses O(N^2) memory in the worst case for the pair map, but typical usage is far lower.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case (at most about 10^6 operations for N=1000).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(P) where P is the number of distinct X‑pairs, bounded by O(N^2).
